text:
Program:The World Today

Presented by

The Paris Olympics are now officially Australia's best games ever, after spectacular performances delivered four gold medals and two bronze in a record-breaking day of competition.

Former Qantas boss Alan Joyce has his payout cut by nearly $10million, following a series of scandals under his reign.

And after a week of rioting in the UK, thousands take to the streets to oppose the anti-immigrant unrest.

Stephanie Smail:Hello and welcome to The World Today. It's Thursday the 8th of August. I'm Stephanie Smale coming to you from the lands of the Turrbul and Yugera people in Brisbane. Today, bumpy landing. Former Qantas boss Alan Joyce has his payout cut by nearly $10 million, following an internal review at the carrier. And after a week of rioting in the UK, thousands take to the streets to oppose the anti-immigrant unrest.

Opinion:We're not going to let fascists, we're not going to let racists and the far right march here.

Stephanie Smail:The Paris Olympics are now officially Australia's best games ever, with spectacular performances delivering four gold medals and two bronze in a record-breaking day of competition. From the ancient sport of pole vaulting to the more modern art of skateboarding, Australia's athletes have pushed the team to third position on the medal tally. To take us through the results and the celebrations, here's Rachel Mealey.

Rachel Mealey:In the wee hours, Australians like Tess Jenkins watched as pole vaulter Nina Kennedy took the nation to new heights of sporting success. As she soared over the 4.9 metre high bar, Nina Kennedy says she knew it was enough to win.

Nina Kennedy:Pole vault's an interesting one because when you clear the bar you just want to let loose and you want to celebrate but you know in the back of my mind I knew these girls weren't going to give up easily and I might have to jump higher so it's about celebrating but switching back on and keeping that focus.

Rachel Mealey:She says competing in front of a crowd of 70,000 intensified the stress of the event.

Nina Kennedy:I think we all knew the winner was going to be the person that could handle the stress of the Olympic Games the best. It is such a mental event.

Rachel Mealey:Nina Kennedy's medal set a new record for Australia with 18 gold medals. Earlier Australian skateboarder Keegan Palmer won his second successive Olympic gold medal at the Men's Park Final. The 21-year-old set the benchmark in the first of his three runs with a score of 93.11, holding off his nearest rivals.

Keegan Palmer:Actually surprisingly enough the first run was more of a safety run to be honest and my last two runs I really wanted to go for some new tricks that I've been holding on to and practicing but sadly fell off in the middle of the run so I didn't really get to get to them but you know we were able to hold on to it with the first run so we definitely take it.

Rachel Mealey:His victory followed another skateboarding medal from 14-year-old Arisa Trew who won gold yesterday in the Women's Park Final. Keegan Palmer says today's gold was a sweet victory.

Keegan Palmer:Dude yeah that was one really crazy feeling that's for sure. It was an absolute honour to be able to skate with all my friends that I've known for the plus 10 years and you know it was emotional slash you know just excited like who would have thought like just the gold was a two-time Olympian and you know now we're a two-time gold medalist it doesn't really get much better than me and especially on the day Australia's had today we broke our record and to be able to be a part of that it's just incredible.

Rachel Mealey:Whether it was on land or on water Australians owned the day. West Australian sailor Matt Wearn also won back-to-back gold in the Men's dinghy competition.

Matt Wearn:These words can't really describe it really it's um yeah pretty phenomenal it's just incredible to be able to go go back to back and bring home a second gold medal.

Rachel Mealey:Australia has now taken out that title for the fourth consecutive Olympics. Matt Wern navigated tricky conditions to seal the win.

Matt Wearn:Just super super variable so there's a lot of clouds going over which is changing the wind a lot a lot of direction changes and strength changes so um yeah it just sort of come and go and yeah it was starting to look like we weren't going to get a race in again today which um which makes it hard to deal with but yeah we managed to do it and yeah it was really really good.

Rachel Mealey:In track cycling Australia claimed gold in the men's team pursuit for the first time since the Athens Olympics 20 years ago. In a blur of speed Sam Welsford, Oliver Bleddyn, Connor Leahy and Kelland O'Brien held off Great Britain to take the win. Darren Casey is from the Bendigo and District Cycling Club. He says he watched in awe.

Darren Casey:Kel is just a real professional he's uh he's the starter of the pursuit team so he does probably the bulk of the power to get the speed up you know like they're sitting on 65, 70 kilometers per hour. So Kel is also a pro tour rider so he lives in Spain so he's got to juggle being a pro tour rider same as Sam Welsford and also a track rider so it's a it's a real juggling act and for him to to get in form a condition to swap from you know 200, 250 kilometer road races and actually get a gold medal is impressive in its own right.

Rachel Mealey:As celebrations continue in Paris back in Sydney Prime Minister Anthony Albanese says the athletes have earned the accolades of the nation.

Anthony Albanese:Because it is uplifting at a time where there's so much conflict and turmoil and bad news in the world what they've given Australians is a great deal of pleasure and pride.

Rachel Mealey:Despite the victories there's been a moment of controversy for Australia. Hockey player Tom Craig was detained by French police. He's been released without charge after trying to purchase cocaine in Paris and will fly home without the Australian team.

Stephanie Smail:Rachel Mealey reporting. The former Qantas boss, Alan Joyce, has had his final payout slashed by nearly $10 million, following a series of major scandals under his leadership. Mr Joyce left Qantas in September last year after 15 years at the helm. Qantas says his pay will be docked because of the airline's high court loss and the recent settlement over flight cancellations. Our business reporter David Taylor joined me earlier. David, why has Alan Joyce's payout been slashed by so much?

David Taylor:Steph, this is essentially the outcome of a performance review by management. Qantas has released the results publicly today via the Stock Exchange. In a statement shared to the ASX, Qantas confirmed Mr Joyce's executive pay would be slashed after the airline lost its high court battle over the illegal sacking of ground staff in 2020 and a recent settlement with the ACCC over flight cancellations. Qantas admitted to misleading customers and, subject to federal court approval, will pay a $100 million penalty. The events, the board said, damaged Qantas and its reputation and caused considerable harm to relationships with customers, employees and other stakeholders. Interestingly, Steph, the board said there were no findings of deliberate wrongdoing by management, but rather mistakes.

Stephanie Smail:And what message is Qantas trying to send with this pay cut?

David Taylor:The message is quite simple. If you make a decision that is not in the interests of the company's stakeholders, and specifically shareholders that give the company money to invest and expand and perform for all stakeholders, you will be financially punished. The question is, is this amount specifically enough to do that? Well, analysts say it is a responsible decision by the board. Here's fund manager Roger Montgomery.

Roger Montgomery:I think the board has done the right thing considering the airline lost its high court battle over what's now being described as an illegal sacking of ground staffback in 2020. So that alone is enough for the review of a salary of any CEO of a company that has been found to act in a way that's inconsistent with a company that wants to maintain its brand reputation.

David Taylor:Just on that though, it's clear that that was ruled illegal and yet the ASX announcement says there was no findings of deliberate wrongdoing by management. How does that add up?

Roger Montgomery:Good question. I think that's a question to ask the company. It doesn't make sense and it's certainly a question for the company to answer.

Stephanie Smail:Fund manager Roger Montgomery there. And David, Australia's biggest companies are about to reveal how profitable they've been over the past 12 months. AMP has reported this morning. What did it have to say?

David Taylor:Well, better news for shareholders because it's had a rocky few years. AMP shares have rocketed up after a solid earnings result. Profit after tax rose 5.4% to $118 million. But Steph, as for upcoming results, global investment make UBS expects corporate Australian earnings to be a fraction lower this time around. It's predicting earnings from ASX listed companies on average will have fallen 3.5% in the past financial year. Of course, that's 2023 to 2024, following a 2.9% drop in the previous year.

Stephanie Smail:David Taylor there. Childcare workers have welcomed a significant pay increase, saying it will help address the staff shortages that have been plaguing the sector. The federal government has announced a 15% wage increase to be rolled out over 12 months from this December. Advocates say the bump in pay is also a major step forward for gender equity, as Kathleen Ferguson reports.

Anthony Albanese:What do you think about my drawing, Ned? Do you think it's good?

Kathleen Ferguson:The Prime Minister, Anthony Albanese, has a crack at some of the crafts which help nurture the country's young.

Anthony Albanese:Hey, what do you reckon?

Opinion:That looks weird. Can I have a look? Can I have a look, Tom?

Kathleen Ferguson:He visited the Sydney Early Childcare Centre after announcing a 15% pay increase for the sector's workers.

Anthony Albanese:They deserve more than our thanks. They deserve decent wages and conditions.

Kathleen Ferguson:Under the deal, workers will get 10% by this Christmas and a further 5% by December next year. It equates to around an extra $100 a week and will cost $3.6 billion. Karen Moran is a long-time educator.

Karen Moran:This decision today will change people's lives. It means that early childhood educators who've been relying on Food Bank to feed their own families won't have to do that anymore. And those that work two and three jobs just to make ends meet will be able to spend more time with their families.

Kathleen Ferguson:But the deal is conditional. In order to receive the pay bump, childcare centres will have to limit their fee increases to 4.4% during the next 12 months. The Minister for Early Childcare, Education and Youth, Dr Anne Aly, says the sector's role in society is critical.

Dr Anne Aly:We know that brain development is... 90% of brain development occurs in those first five years. This is not babysitting, this is not wiping bums and noses. This is education. We see you, we hear you, we acknowledge you. We know the vital and professional work that you do.

Kathleen Ferguson:The early childcare sector says it's been a long and hard-fought battle. Georgie Dent is the CEO of advocacy group The Parenthood.

Georgie Dent:This is a long overdue recognition of the incredible contribution that early childhood educators make to our children, to our communities, to families. It is such a fantastic achievement.

Kathleen Ferguson:Georgie Dent says this pay increase is desperately needed to combat a worker shortage in the sector, which is mostly made up of women.

Georgie Dent:The sector is overjoyed. This has been a long-realised dream. We have known that early childhood educators have been chronically underpaid and undervalued. We have seen in recent years the accelerating exit of early educators from the sector, which has put incredible pressure on services.

Kathleen Ferguson:This is an interim measure which will be in place for two years while the federal government reviews the sector. The federal education minister, Jason Clare, says it will be up to childcare centres and providers to sign on to the scheme. And he says if centres don't sign up, they should be prepared to lose staff to competitors.

Jason Clare:This is a cost-of-living win-win. It's good for workers and it's good for parents. And at its heart, this is good for education, for the next generation, setting our kids up for success.

Kathleen Ferguson:Shadow Treasurer Angus Taylor supports higher wages but says bringing down inflation is the best way to increase pay.

Angus Taylor:Well, we want to see higher real wages, which means lower prices for everything, for all Australians, PK. And we'll look at the detail of this proposal that Labor's putting forward. If it can raise real wages for Australians, of course we're going to support it. That's our position.

Kathleen Ferguson:The United Workers Union says the increase sends a strong message to women. Jo Schofield is the union's national president.

Jo Schofield:Just this week, we did a survey that showed 60% of educators wanted to leave within three years. 97% of centres have seen educators walk out in the last year, and the single factor in that is the low pay.

Kathleen Ferguson:She says while the union will continue advocating for better pay and conditions, this is a good start.

Stephanie Smail:Kathleen Ferguson reporting. On ABC Radio across Australia, streaming online, and on the ABC Listen app this is The World Today, thanks for your company. Thousands of people have streamed onto the streets in cities across the United Kingdom to share messages of peace amid ongoing race riots. Counter-protesters are taking a stand as police battle widespread violence targeting Muslims and immigrants. The unrest has been triggered by false information about the fatal stabbing of three children in the town of Southport. Alexandra Humphries reports.

Alexandra Humphries:After a week of chaos, Britons are taking a stand. Thousands taking to the streets to protest the anti-immigrant unrest that swept the nation. Many here in London are holding signs with phrases like refugees welcome and no room for Nazis. We're

Opinion:We're not going to let fascists, we're not going to let racists and the far right march here.

Opinion:My parents' generation went through great sacrifices to fight against fascism from Germany and it started off with small groups on the streets attacking immigrants or in those days Jews. Now the same types of people are on the streets in this country. It's absolutely disgraceful and I'm standing here against that.

Alexandra Humphries:Far right groups were reportedly planning to target asylum centres and immigration lawyers across Britain overnight. Businesses have closed early, shopkeepers have also boarded up their premises. For these workers in the northern English town of Oldham, there's real concern.

Opinion:People are scared, yeah. A lot of customers coming in, looking over their shoulder. A lot of people walking past looking over their shoulders. You can see they're frightened, yeah.

Opinion:We're upholsterers, we work down here in the mill down here. Like I say, everyone's evacuated and that.

Opinion:I don't want to say it but we're shut down so we're not earning nothing.

Alexandra Humphries:Some 6,000 specialist officers have been deployed to curb the unrest. London Metropolitan Police Commissioner Mark Rowley described the mobilisation as a standing army.

Mark Rowley:We've changed our tactics in the last couple of days. So besides marshalling more officers, we're brigading some of them at key strategic locations across the country to give us more agility to respond to wherever the protests are most intense.

Alexandra Humphries:It's been the worst outbreak of violence in Britain in more than a decade, sparked by false information spread after the stabbing deaths of three young girls at a dance class in Southport. Meanwhile, Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner is reassuring the public that the situation can be controlled.

Angela Rayner:What has been heartening is to see the community response. The vast majority of the British people see this for what it is, that it is criminality, it is thuggish behaviour. It is not a legitimate complaint to throw bricks at police and to try and set fire to public buildings.

Lord Michael Howard:We are witnessing lawless disorder which can't be tolerated, which must be met with the full force of the law and is being met with the full force of the law.

Alexandra Humphries:Lord Michael Howard is a former Home Secretary of the United Kingdom.

Lord Michael Howard:There is undoubtedly a lot of pent-up frustration at various things, including particularly the level of immigration. We have had unprecedented levels of immigration in recent years and that is something that people are frustrated about. But it is absolutely no excuse whatever for the kind of lawless behaviour that we've been seeing.

Alexandra Humphries:Three men have already received prison sentences for their roles in the riots in Southport and Liverpool.

Stephanie Smail:Alexandra Humphries there. The Russian President Vladimir Putin has accused Ukraine of a large-scale provocation, following reports Ukrainian troops have made their biggest advance into Russian territory since the war began. The Russian military says it stopped Ukraine's incursion into the southwestern Kursk region, but heavy fighting appears to be continuing, as Declan Gooch reports.

Declan Gooch:Russian President Vladimir Putin has declared a state of emergency in his country's southwest.

Vladimir Putin:The Kiev regime has undertaken another large-scale provocation and launched indiscriminate shelling of civilian buildings, residential houses, ambulances with different types of weapons.

Declan Gooch:The Russian Defence Ministry says hundreds of Ukrainian troops, supported by more than two dozen armoured vehicles and tanks, crossed into Russia's Kursk region on Tuesday morning. While Ukraine is yet to confirm any details, the Russian Foreign Ministry says Ukrainian shelling killed at least two people and injured 24. Armed Forces Chief of Staff Valery Gerasimov claims Russian troops stopped a Ukrainian advance.

Valery Gerasimov:Russian troops continue to destroy the enemy in areas directly adjacent to the Russian-Ukrainian border. The enemy's losses amount to 315 people, including at least 100 killed and 215 wounded.

Declan Gooch:But on social media, Kursk Governor Alexei Smirnov has urged residents to donate blood due to ongoing intense fighting, and he said authorities had evacuated thousands of people from the region.

Alexei Smirnov:Overnight, new temporary accommodation centres with more than 2,500 places have been deployed in the region, and more than 300 people, including 121 children, have been placed in them.

Declan Gooch:The fighting is near a major gas hub that serves as the only point of entry for Russian gas into the EU via Ukraine, which has continued to operate despite the war. White House spokeswoman Karine Jean-Pierre says Ukraine gave no advance warning of the operation.

Karine Jean-Pierre:We're going to reach out to them to see what their objective is and continue to stay focused. We are going to continue to stay focused on making sure they have what they need to defend themselves against Russia's aggression.

Declan Gooch:Ukrainian incursions into Russia have been very rare since the war began in February 2022.

Malcolm Davis:Previously, we have had essentially Russian forces or Russian units that were loyal to Ukraine fighting a conflict there, but this is the first time where we've seen actual Ukrainian military forces go across the border.

Declan Gooch:Malcolm Davis is a senior analyst at the Australian Strategic Policy Institute.

Malcolm Davis:I think it is quite significant given the size of the intervention and the open question of how far did they go?

Declan Gooch:Malcolm Davis says the incursion is probably about more than just inflicting damage.

Malcolm Davis:My guess is that the Ukrainians would then try to reinforce their position to hold onto the captured territory.

Declan Gooch:What might be the overarching strategy here?

Malcolm Davis:The broader strategy is to try and deny the Russians essentially sanctuaries from which they can attack Ukraine. If this offensive is successful, then the Ukrainians will control some key areas of territory inside Russia that is then denied to the Russians.

Declan Gooch:While it's unlikely to represent a major turning point in the war, he says it will have political implications for Vladimir Putin.

Malcolm Davis:Obviously, it's a humiliation for him. I don't see him being overthrown as a result of this, but he's more likely to be a little bit more reckless in terms of how he responds. He could potentially escalate elsewhere.

Stephanie Smail:Malcolm Davis from the Australian Strategic Policy Institute, ending that report by Declan Gooch.New research shows the waters around the Great Barrier Reef have hit their hottest summer temperatures in 400 years. Scientists say climate change is to blame and they're calling for more action to protect the World Heritage-listed region. Elizabeth Cramsie reports.

Elizabeth Cramsie:Alan Wallish has been operating Great Barrier Reef tours from Cairns for over 35 years and the founder and director of Passions of Paradise says conditions appear as good as he's ever seen them.

Alan Wallish:It is very colourful, it appears to be in a very, very healthy condition. So we suffered very little bleaching off the Cairns Port Douglas region this year and what we did have a little bit, it has recovered but overall the reef is looking in really good condition.

Elizabeth Cramsie:But scientists say the habitat is being pushed closer to a tipping point from which it may not recover. New research shows under the surface temperatures are the warmest they've been in 400 years.

Dr Ben Henley:Our study shows that the recent events are very extreme in that long-term context and we also use climate models to confirm that climate change is responsible for the warming and extreme high sea surface temperature events.

Elizabeth Cramsie:That's Dr Ben Henley from the University of Wollongong. He's the lead researcher. The study focused on periods from January to March using already published records from coral colonies.

Dr Ben Henley:And these are cores that are drilled into these coral colonies and the chemistry, the chemical signatures that are laid down at the time the coral was growing, the coral surface is alive and the interior of the coral colony is a dead skeleton. They record the temperature and other environmental conditions at the time it's growing so we can take those cores back to the lab and our colleagues over the previous decades from around the region have published these fantastic records, some of them at very high resolution, going back in time for up to 400 years or so.

Elizabeth Cramsie:And the results were so shocking he had to check them twice.

Dr Ben Henley:I had to actually plot it again and have a careful check of the year because it was just so extreme it really just jumped off the top of the graph. It is the warmest on record in the last 400 years.

Elizabeth Cramsie:He says while good work is being done, the reef needs more.

Dr Ben Henley:We have to deal with climate change and it's the long term driver of the risk to the reef.

Elizabeth Cramsie:Dr Lissa Schindler is from the Australian Marine Conservation Society.

Dr Lissa Schindler:It was the highest and most prolonged and extreme temperatures that the Great Barrier Reef has experienced and that's why we saw such huge impact from a bleaching perspective.

Elizabeth Cramsie:Alan Wallish says it's not all doom and gloom but ensuring the ongoing health of the reef will take a combined effort.

Alan Wallish:The Great Barrier Reef is not one thing. It's a huge living organism that's over 2000km long and what happens on one section might not necessarily happen on another. It is a little bit of a canary in the coal mine. So we do want to protect our precious resource here and we need to be working together, science, tourism operators, the communities, traditional owners etc. This is an all encompassing thing that we need to do.

Stephanie Smail:Great Barrier Reef tour operator Alan Wallish ending that report by Elizabeth Cramsie. And that's all from the World Today team. Thanks for your company. I'm Stephanie Smail.

title: Australia smashes Olympic medal record - ABC listen
description: The Paris Olympics are now officially Australia's best games ever, after spectacular performances delivered four gold medals and two bronze in a record-breaking day of competition.
